structure.title: Preface
structure.summary: This preface section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" document provides an overview and essential information regarding the raw data stream specification within the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. It introduces key concepts such as RdsErrorDomain, AVTPDU headers, and various interfaces for raw data streaming using IP-based protocols (like IEEE 1722). The preface outlines important relationships between different components and processes involved in raw data streaming, including security measures like access control via IAM and secure communication. Additionally, it details the document's history, copyright information, and constraints applicable to its use.

structure.title: Introduction and functional overview
structure.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" document introduces and describes the functionality, API, and configuration for handling raw binary data streams in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. Key concepts include the distinction between byte stream and datagram stream processing, with support for both TCP/IP and IEEE1722 protocols. The integration is defined during deployment through specific attributes and parameters, and secure communication can be achieved using TLS/IPSec or MACSec protocols. Important relationships are established between the Raw Data Streaming Interface and adaptive applications, while constraints involve the selection of appropriate transport protocols based on application requirements.

structure.title: Acronyms and Abbreviations
structure.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" document outlines a glossary of acronyms and abbreviations specific to this technical specification. Key concepts include network protocols such as IP, SOME/IP, TCP, and UDP, which are essential for data transmission. Additionally, it defines various formats like RVF (Raw Video Format), CRF (Control Reference Format), TSCF (Time Sensitive Control Format), and NTSCF (None Time Sensitive Control Format) used in the context of raw data streams. The document also references standards such as IEC 61883/IIDC, IEEE1722, and AVTP, establishing a framework for understanding the technical details and relationships between different components involved in the transmission and processing of raw data.

nodes.title: Definitions
nodes.summary: The "Definitions" section in the "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" outlines key terms and their meanings essential for understanding the document. It introduces fundamental concepts such as "raw data stream," "data packet," and "timestamp," which are crucial for interpreting the technical specifications. nodes.title: MAC unicast socket connection
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" document explains the concept of a "MAC unicast socket connection," which is a type of communication established via a socket provided by the operating system and bound to the data link layer (ISO OSI layer 2). The key component here is the media access control (MAC) address, which uniquely identifies one device on a network segment. This connection allows for point-to-point communication with exactly one other device (unicast), ensuring that data sent from one end is received by only the intended recipient. While no specific tables are mentioned in this excerpt, it emphasizes the importance of addressing and routing at the data link layer to facilitate unicast communication over a network.

nodes.title: MAC multicast socket connection
nodes.summary: This section of "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" focuses on the concept of a "MAC multicast socket connection," which involves communication through a socket interface at the data link layer (ISO OSI layer 2) using media access control (MAC) addresses. Key components include the socket provided by the operating system and the ability to reach multiple communication partners simultaneously via multicast. The section emphasizes that this type of connection is bound to the lower layers of the network protocol stack, facilitating efficient data transmission to a group of devices sharing the same network segment. While no specific tables are mentioned in the excerpt, these details provide foundational knowledge for understanding how multicast operations are implemented at the MAC level.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-common-header
nodes.summary: This section of "AVTPDU-common-header" describes the initial 12 bits of an Audio/Video Transport Protocol Data Unit (AVTPDU) header as defined in IEEE1722. It consists of three main components: subtype (8 bits), which identifies the specific format of AVTP stream data; header specific (1 bit), indicating additional information within the header; and version (3 bits), specifying the protocol version. These fields are common across all subtypes of AVTP stream data, ensuring consistency in how different types of audio/video data are transmitted. While no tables are explicitly mentioned, these components form the foundational structure for understanding and implementing AVTPDU headers in various applications.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-common-stream-header
nodes.summary: This section of "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" focuses on the "AVTPDU-common-stream-header," which is defined in IEEE1722 and extends the AVTPDU-common-header for specific types of AVTP stream data subtypes, such as 61883_IIDC, AAF, TSCF, and RVF. The key concept here involves expanding the header to accommodate these particular data subtypes, thereby enhancing their functionality within the AVTP (Audio Video Transport Protocol) framework. While no tables are explicitly mentioned in this excerpt, it is implied that there are detailed specifications for how these headers are structured and used, which would typically include fields such as stream identifiers, sequence numbers, and error correction mechanisms to ensure data integrity and efficient transmission.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-common-control-header
nodes.summary: This section of "AVTPDU-common-control-header" discusses the definition and usage of an "AVTPDU-common-stream-header," as specified in [1, IEEE1722]. This header is an extension of the AVTPDU-common-header used for a subset of AVTP stream data subtypes, such as Audio Data Packets (ADP). The section outlines how this header facilitates more specific control and management within audio/video transport protocols. While no tables are explicitly mentioned in the provided content, it implies that there are underlying technical details regarding the structure and function of these headers that would be detailed elsewhere in the document.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-alternative-header
nodes.summary: This section of "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" discusses the AVTPDU-alternative-header, a specific header format defined in IEEE1722 for handling subtypes of AVTP stream data that do not share common characteristics with other formats using either the AVTPDU-common-stream-header or AVTPDU-common-controlheaders. Key examples include CRF and NTSCF subtypes, which utilize this alternative header structure. While no tables are explicitly mentioned in the provided content, it highlights the differentiation and application of this header format to ensure proper handling of diverse stream data types within the AVTP protocol framework.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-payload
nodes.summary: This section of "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" focuses on the definition and role of the AVTPDU-payload within the context of AVTP (Audio Video Transport Protocol) data units. Key concepts include that an AVTPDU-payload is defined by IEEE1722 and constitutes the second part of an AVTPDU, carrying data encoded according to the subtype specified in the AVTPDU-header. For instance, it can carry audio data such as audio samples encapsulated within an AAF (Audio Audio Frame) payload. While no specific tables are mentioned, this section establishes a foundational understanding of how different types of payloads are structured and utilized within AVTP for transporting multimedia content efficiently.

nodes.title: Stream data producer
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" document focuses on defining and explaining the role of a "stream data producer," which is an end node in an Ethernet network responsible for continuously generating data. This data is then transmitted via a stream and received by one or multiple "stream data consumers." The term "talker" is used interchangeably with "stream data producer," highlighting its active role in initiating the data transmission process. While no specific tables are mentioned, this section establishes foundational terminology that will likely be referenced throughout the document to describe the interactions between producers and consumers within the network architecture.

nodes.title: Stream data consumer
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" document focuses on defining and explaining the concept of a "stream data consumer," which is an essential component in Ethernet networks. It continuously consumes data received via streams, with this term being synonymous to "listener." The definition highlights the role of these consumers as end nodes within the network architecture, emphasizing their continuous data intake process. While no specific tables are mentioned in the provided content, the section establishes foundational terminology and roles crucial for understanding how data flows and is processed in Ethernet networks.

nodes.title: AVTP presentation time
nodes.summary: This section of "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" defines the concept of AVTP presentation time, a critical timing mechanism in audio/video transport protocol (AVTP) systems. It explains that this time is defined by IEEE1722 and represents the gPTP (Grandmaster Precision Time Protocol) timestamp at which designated data within an AVTPDU payload is transferred to a time-sensitive application of a stream data consumer. The presentation time is calculated as the sum of the current global time and the maximum transit time, denoted as "TavtpPresentationTime" = "TcurrentGlobalTime" + "TmaxTransitTime". Importantly, this definition excludes format conversion time and processing time by the receiving application, as illustrated in IEEE1722's Figure 6.

nodes.title: Max transit time
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" document discusses the concept of "Max transit time," which is defined according to IEEE1722 standards. It explains that this value is determined by identifying the worst-case transit time between a data producer (referred to as a talker) and a data consumer (known as a listener), then setting the max transit time to be at least equal to the longest of these worst-case times. This ensures reliable data transmission, with no delays exceeding the specified limit. While no tables are explicitly mentioned in this excerpt, the process described is crucial for ensuring consistent and timely data flow in networked systems.

nodes.title: Media clock
nodes.summary: This section of "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" focuses on the definition and role of a "Media clock," which is defined in IEEE1722. The media clock generates a precise rate, such as 48kHz, typically through a hardware mechanism. It operates under the management of a media clock provider, highlighting its dependency and integration within the broader system architecture. While no specific tables are mentioned, this section emphasizes the importance of maintaining accurate timing for data streams, which is crucial for synchronization in multimedia applications.

nodes.title: Media clock provider
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" document defines a "media clock provider," which is an end node in the network responsible for hosting and transmitting an IEEE1722 CRF (clock reference format) stream to one or multiple media clock consumers. The key concept here involves the transmission of presentation timestamps within this stream, which are correlated to the media clock rate. This setup ensures synchronization between different nodes in a network by providing a consistent time reference for media content. While no specific tables are mentioned, the IEEE1722 standard and its CRF subtype play crucial roles in facilitating accurate timing and synchronization processes.

nodes.title: Media clock consumer
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" document defines a key component in media streaming networks—the "media clock consumer." It explains that this end node receives a CRF (clock reference format) stream from a media clock provider and uses it to recover its internal media clock, typically through a phase-locked loop (PLL). The process involves the media clock consumer interpreting encapsulated data sent by the provider to synchronize its operations. While no tables are explicitly mentioned in this excerpt, the section emphasizes the critical role of synchronization between different nodes in the network for maintaining consistent timing and ensuring smooth media delivery.

nodes.title: ACF-stream
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" document defines an "ACF-stream," which is a type of IEEE1722 stream that can be either time-synchronous (TSCF) or non-time-synchronous (NTSCF). It primarily transports encapsulated bus frames as ACF-messages, such as ACF_CAN, within its AVTPDU-payload. The key components include the stream subtype and the specific message formats it supports. This definition establishes a clear relationship between the stream type and the content it carries, emphasizing that the data is structured in ACF-messages for transmission.

nodes.title: ACF-message
nodes.summary: This section of "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" explains the concept of an ACF-message, which is defined according to IEEE1722 and represents a bus frame encapsulated for specific types such as CAN. Key components include the ACF-message-header and payload, with multiple ACF-messages potentially forming an ACF-payload within an ACF-stream. The document outlines that these messages can vary in type but are unified under the broader concept of ACF-streams for data transport.

nodes.title: ACF-message-header
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" document describes the structure and components of an ACF-message-header. It explains that the header consists of two main parts: the first 7 bits define the type of ACF-message, which can include types such as ACF_CAN or ACF_LIN as specified in IEEE1722 [1]; the subsequent 9 bits indicate the length of the message payload that follows. This structure allows for a clear and standardized way to identify and process different types of messages within an ACF (Automotive Communication Framework) system, ensuring efficient data transmission and interpretation.

nodes.title: ACF-message-payload
nodes.summary: This section of "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" focuses on the definition and characteristics of the "ACF-message-payload," which is a critical component of an ACF-message as per IEEE1722 standards. The payload carries encoded data specific to the type of ACF message indicated by the header, such as CAN2.0 frames for ACF_CANpayloads. This structure ensures that the appropriate data format and content are transmitted according to the message type, facilitating efficient and accurate communication in the specified technical context.

nodes.title: Raw Data Streaming
nodes.summary: This section of "Raw Data Streaming" describes the API for managing raw data streams in C++, focusing on the operations and behaviors associated with establishing and maintaining communication links. The section also outlines timeout handling and the destructor's behavior when no explicit `Shutdown` is performed. Importantly, it specifies that operations such as `Connect` and `WaitForConnection` are only applicable to TCP connections, while UDP connections do not require these operations. Additionally, the summary notes that the `WriteData` operation does not guarantee data order for UDP connections, relying on underlying network protocols for proper transmission.

nodes.title: Access Control via IAM
nodes.summary: This section discusses access control mechanisms via Identity and Access Management (IAM) for the creation of `RawDataStream` clients and servers. It introduces key concepts such as `InstanceSpecifier`, `RPortPrototype`, and `RawDataStreamMapping`. The document outlines a specific constraint where creating a `RawDataStreamClient` or `RawDataStreamServer` with an `InstanceSpecifier` that does not match any `RPortPrototype` mapped to the requesting process is considered a violation. This ensures that only processes authorized through IAM can access specific data streams, thereby maintaining security and integrity in data processing systems.

nodes.title: Secure Communication
nodes.summary: This section discusses the security mechanisms available for securing Raw Data Stream communication over TCP and UDP. It introduces three key protocols: TLS 1.2, DTLS 1.2, and IPSec. The relationships between these protocols are highlighted, indicating that they can be used to ensure secure data transmission depending on the transport protocol (TCP or UDP). Technical details include references to relevant RFCs for TLS 1.2 ([RFC5246]) and DTLS 1.2 ([RFC6347]), providing a basis for understanding the implementation standards.

nodes.title: Creation and use of secure channels for Raw Data Streaming
nodes.summary: This section discusses the creation and use of secure channels for raw data streaming using TLS (Transport Layer Security) and DTLS (Datagram Transport Layer Security). Key concepts include the requirement that the Raw Data Stream software must establish secure UDP and TCP channels based on specified `TlsSecureComProps` as part of the `EthernetRawDataStreamMapping`. It also outlines that all communication initiated by a `RawDataStream` should be transmitted via these defined secure channels, with the appropriate channel being determined from the `TlsSecureComProps` associated with the `EthernetCommunicationConnector`. The section references upstream requirements RS_CM_00801 and RS_CM_00803 to ensure compliance.

nodes.title: (D)TLS for Raw Data Streaming
nodes.summary: This section of "Specification of Raw Data Stream.pdf" discusses the use of (D)TLS and DTLS for securing raw data streams in network communications. Key concepts include TLS and DTLS cipher suites such as `TLS_PSK_WITH_NULL_SHA256` for authenticity, and `TLS_PSK_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256` for confidentiality. The section outlines requirements for creating secure channels based on the transport protocol used: TCP or UDP. For reliable transport over TCP, a TLS secure channel is mandated if an instance of `TlsSecureComProps` is configured in an `EthernetRawDataStreamMapping`. Conversely, for unreliable transport over UDP, a DTLS secure channel must be established under similar conditions. Additionally, it mentions the use of IPsec as another method to secure raw data streams, referencing specific requirements and constraints from other sections of the document.
